The initial integration budget for the Discover acquisition was estimated at $2.8 billion per the Q2, 2025 conference call. While the firm has already incurred a large portion of these costs, $234 million in 2024 and $1.1 billion in 2025 per the 2025 10-K, the CEO, Richard Fairbank, has stated that these costs are now expected to come in "somewhat higher" per the Q2, 2025 conference call. Even holding the acquisition costs at $2.8 billion means the firm has at least $1.46 billion of Discover integration costs to incur for 2026 and beyond. In addition to the integration costs, Capital One will amortize roughly $1.9 billion in 2026 for intangibles related to Discover per the Q4, 2025 earnings presentation.

Moreover, Capital One has entered into an agreement to acquire Brex, a cloud-based, AI-native platform that integrates corporate credit cards, expense management, and banking services into a single software system. The acquisition will use $2.58 billion in cash and 10.6 million shares of Capital One common stock per the Q4, 2025 earnings presentation. Management expects to incur about $950 million in transaction costs over the next three years to cover the acquisition of Brex. Additionally, Capital One expects to have amortization of capitalized software totaling $500 million over the next three years and amortization of other intangibles totaling $350 million over the next five years. For Brex, the total annual pre-tax expense drag is roughly $554 million per year for the first three years. With roughly 625 million shares outstanding and an approximate 24% effective tax rate, the annual after-tax drag is about $421 million, or $0.67/share annually. The deal is expected to close mid-2026; therefore, the firm will only absorb half this drag, or about $0.33 per share.

The future integration costs and amortization will continue to depress earnings for the next 12 to 24 months. While management will likely continue to point to adjusted earnings per share going forward, the market will begin to discount this as the integration has become a structural issue at this point.

Deteriorating Macro

The next argument for my hold thesis rests on the deterioration in the broader macroeconomic environment from rates being held higher for longer, rising consumer delinquencies, and weaker labor market conditions.

As of the time of this writing, Fed Funds Futures are pricing in a 75% chance of no rate cut in 2026 and only a 20% chance of one 25 basis point cut per the CME FedWatch tool. Banks benefit most from a steepening yield curve, short rates down, and long rates up. With the market now pricing short-term rates to stay higher for longer, the yield curve may not steepen as expected. Consequently, the cost to fund deposits (the primary funding source of loans) will be higher than expected, which compresses net interest margin.

Next, there are signs of consumer credit conditions deteriorating across the board. First, the New York Fed's Quarterly Report on Household Debt and Credit showed household debt rose to $18.8 trillion in Q4, 2024, while 4.8% of the outstanding debt was in some stage of delinquency, the highest rate since 2017. Moreover, the portion of student loans transitioning to serious delinquency (greater than 90 days past due) jumped to 16.19% in Q4, 2025, an all-time high. While Capital One does not have exposure to student loans, the jump in delinquencies can be a warning sign of consumer hardship. Additionally, delinquency in student loans can drop FICO scores by 49 to 82 points per VantageScore. Falling FICO scores may make it more difficult for these consumers to qualify for new credit cards, auto loans, or personal loans - all products offered by Capital One.

Further, according to Capital One's filings, the 30+ day delinquency rate in domestic cards has been rising following the Discover acquisition. Domestic card delinquencies rose from 3.60% in Q2, 2025, to 3.89% in Q3, 2025, to 3.99% in Q4, 2025. At the same time, the firm's Domestic Card Allowance Coverage Ratio, which measures the ACL versus the annualized net charge-offs, has been decreasing following the acquisition (5.43% in Q2, 2025, to 5.16% in Q4, 2025). Compounding this issue, management released $753 million from the Domestic Card reserve in Q3, 2025, at the same time the delinquency rate was rising. Management defended this by pointing to "continued observed credit favorability in both losses and recovered" and a slightly improved macroeconomic unemployment forecast per the Q3, 2025 earnings call transcript. The contradiction of releasing reserves citing credit favorability while delinquencies are ticking up further shows investors should approach the stock with caution.

Lastly, the labor market has shown signs of softening over the last six months. The March Employment Situation showed the U.S. added 178,000 jobs in March 2026, and the unemployment rate fell to 4.3%. However, the 178k headline hides 76k healthcare jobs, 35k of which were healthcare workers returning from strikes in February. Additionally, according to RBC Economics, the U.S. economy added only 15,000 jobs per month over the prior six months, with the bulk of the gains coming from healthcare. During this six-month period, healthcare added an average of 85,000 jobs. Therefore, without the healthcare sector, the U.S. has been losing jobs for the last six months. These facts matter for Capital One because unemployment shrinks deposits and destroys consumer lending growth.

Valuation

The final reason for the hold thesis is the current valuation for Capital One showing little room for upside potential. Price-to-Tangible Book Value (P/TBV) is the preferred metric for financial firms as it controls for intangible assets like goodwill, which result from non-core activities such as acquisitions. We will use price-to-earnings (P/E) as a backup metric, although it can be distorted for financial firms by the provision for credit losses line item on the income statement.

On a P/TBV level, Capital One is trading at 1.79 versus the peer median of 1.87. Using the median to derive a justified price on a P/TBV level, we arrive at a price of $202.78, implying Capital One is trading at roughly a 5% discount to the justified price with the current price of $192.71 as of the time of this writing. Therefore, on a P/TBV level, Capital One appears to be slightly undervalued with minimal upside potential.

As a backup metric, we will now use the P/E ratio. On a P/E basis, Capital One trades at 11.15 versus the peer median of 11.53, a slight discount. However, using the peer median to derive a justified price shows that Capital One trades at an approximate 8% premium to the justified price of $178.10 on a P/E level. The P/E ratio valuation further shows minimal, if any, upside to the stock at these prices.

Risks

The hold thesis for Capital One comes with inherent risks, including integration delays, worsening credit conditions, regulatory hurdles, and a possible recession hitting subprime harder than normal.

Integration Delays

Any possible delays in the final integrations with Discover or the upcoming integration with Brex may impair earnings further or cause unexpected expenses to arise, putting downward pressure on the share price.

Credit Deterioration Accelerates

If the consumer credit conditions destabilize further or unemployment spikes, charge-offs could exceed current levels, putting downward pressure on earnings.

Regulatory Hurdles

With the acquisition of Discover, Capital One is one of the largest credit card issuers by payment volume and now owns a payment network. Brex serves roughly one in three U.S. startups, and its acquisition may draw antitrust scrutiny from regulators.

Recession

A recession in the U.S. would adversely affect Capital One's large subprime credit card and auto loan portfolio. Subprime delinquencies tend to rise faster during economic stress scenarios due to subprime borrowers having higher debt-to-income ratios and less savings to absorb financial impacts.

Conclusion

Capital One is a top-tier financial services company that has transformed itself into a vertically integrated payments network with the acquisition of Discover. However, I am placing a hold rating on the firm based on the continued integration costs dampening future earnings, signs of consumer stress spreading throughout the economy, and the current valuation levels showing little room for upside growth. I will re-evaluate my position if the planned integrations are smoother than expected, the macroeconomic outlook improves, or the stock trades at a price that makes valuations more attractive.

data.title: Markets May Be Pricing In The Wrong Outcome
data.description:
The stock and credit markets decided that the conflict in the Middle East had ended this week. Whether that was right or wrong, we simply do not know yet. Certainly, the headlines do not suggest it has ended, nor does the price of oil.

Stocks had a strong week, with the S&P 500 rising by more than 3.5%, while oil prices fell by more than 14%. This led to financial conditions easing. At least on the surface, it appeared as if the problems and fears of the Middle East conflict were behind us.

Oil's Impact on Markets

The one remaining issue is the price of oil itself, which is still trading at nearly $100 per barrel, and the longer it persists, the more likely it is to inflict more damage on markets and the global economy as financial conditions actually tighten.

Financial conditions are the mechanism that transfers higher oil prices into the market and the global economy. Markets automatically adjust to higher oil by pricing in higher future inflation rates, which cause interest rates to rise, credit spreads to widen, dollar funding to grow more costly, reducing market liquidity, and causing stocks to sink.

This past week, that trend reversed as oil was modestly lower. We saw this same thing happen in 2022 pretty often, with oil prices surging and spreads such as the Italian and German 10-year rates widening, but they often lagged the movement in oil prices.

This time around, we have seen oil prices move higher much more quickly than in 2022, while credit spread movements have been delayed and have not moved nearly as far or as fast. There could be a few reasons for this, with the most obvious being that the credit and rates markets still believe there will be a quick resolution to the conflict in the Middle East, and that oil prices will fall fast, inflicting minimal damage on the economy. But one would think that the longer the conflict continues, the more likely credit spreads are to widen.

Inflation Not Likely To Persist

On the surface, CPI swaps seem to think the current spike in oil prices and inflation will be transitory, with 1-year CPI swaps trading at 3.06% and 5-year CPI swaps trading at 2.49%.

Overall, the driver of the stock market rally over the past week was the fall in oil prices and the easing of financial conditions. It serves as a strong example of the market looking through the impacts of oil. We saw these stop-and-start points in 2022 a number of times, so it's worth being aware that the same may be happening again.

Not So Fast

With the market basically pricing in the end of the conflict and oil having a minimal longer-term impact. The question that remains is where does oil eventually and actually settle out, because where it settles out tells what the Fed will do and, in essence, may bring with it the second round of tightening financial conditions as the market begins to price in the impacts of higher inflation, and higher Fed Funds rates, which in turn push real rates up, which tighten financial conditions.

Right now, we may be in a transition period, where the market is trying to price in the end of the conflict in the Middle East, but because we do not know where oil is going, there are still very large tail risks on the horizon. If this is a repeat of the 2022 oil shock, the market is more likely to be too optimistic at this stage and hasn't yet priced in an oil price that isn't likely to come down quickly.

If it turns out that there will be no quick resolution to this war, then gains in markets will likely unwind very quickly.

data.title: Garmin: Heady Multiples That Can't Be Justified
data.description:
As we navigate a choppy stock market in 2026, stock selection remains of the utmost importance. With the S&P 500 roughly flat again since the start of the year, we can no longer count on a rising tide to lift all boats. And what we have to be particularly careful of are stocks that are sitting at premium multiples, in spite of potential macro headwinds.

To me, Garmin (GRMN) is one of these stocks. The maker of fitness smartwatches, navigational equipment, and GPS products has seen its share price surge ~30% since the start of this year, ignoring weakness elsewhere in the market. The question for investors now is, can the rally really keep going?

There’s no denying that Garmin is picking up market share and achieving very strong sales, especially in fitness watches. At the same time, the company is up against rising product costs (tariffs plus memory prices) and a potentially shaky macro. That would be fine if Garmin were trading at average market multiples. But after the sharp run-up over the past year, the stock’s premium is undeniably fragile. I’m initiating Garmin with a sell rating.

Broad Lineup of Active Products

Before we dig into why Garmin’s valuation makes the recent rally vulnerable, let’s first dig through a bird’s-eye overview of the company and its products.

Garmin has five primary product segments: fitness, outdoor, marine, aviation, and auto. The scale of these segments and their revenue in FY25 is shown in the snapshot below:

For those of us who haven’t caught up with recent trends, it’s easy to still think of the Garmin of the past, which was primarily known for its navigational GPS systems. While the company still retains its heritage products, the primary growth engine for Garmin now is fitness. Alongside Apple Watch (AAPL) and Fitbit, Garmin is one of the leading smartwatch manufacturers in the U.S. and is increasingly boosting its share overseas as well.

The fitness segment represented one-third of Garmin’s revenue in FY25; moreover, revenue in this segment soared 33% y/y for the full year FY25, with sales actually accelerating in the most recent quarter. Fitness also tends to be the company’s higher-margin product segment, with this division generating the lion’s share (39%) of overall company operating profits.

The snapshot below, taken from Garmin’s website, showcases the company’s core features product lineup. Important to note here is that Garmin’s lineup spans price points.

The company’s Forerunner 165 Watch represents its entry lineup for runners’ watches, starting at $250. The Forerunner lineup goes up to the 970 model, priced at $750. Higher-end customers can trade up to the $900 Enduro 3 model, featuring 320 hours of battery life, preloaded offline maps, a flashlight, and a lightweight design.

Needless to say, Garmin isn’t alone in the fitness watch category. Apple dominates the market and also has a diverse product lineup, ranging from the high-end Ultra ($799) to the standard Series 11 ($399) and entry-level SE ($249). Garmin tends to attract a more serious fitness crowd, as its watches offer state-of-the-art tracking, as well as outdoor adventurers who prize Garmin’s navigational capabilities plus its super long battery lifespan. Apple, meanwhile, wins many over on design as well as integration into the Apple walled garden and connectivity with messaging, email, and other non-fitness functions.

Beyond Fitness, the company has a wide span of other products. The Outdoor segment is Garmin’s second largest, representing 28% of revenue and an also-large 37% chunk of operating profits. That said, growth in this segment has been more tepid, with only 5% growth recorded in FY25. Operating profit in this division also fell -2% y/y last year.

A showcase of the company’s navigation and communications products is shown below:

We note that the company is banking on the Outdoor product to experience accelerating revenue in FY26, on the back of meaningful product launches planned for the back half of this year.

Outside of Fitness and Outdoor, the company’s other segments are Marine, Aviation, and Auto. The marine and aviation segments also offer technical and navigational hardware products, alongside a lineup of very high-end aviation and diving watches (in the $1,000+ price point range). The company’s Auto segment, meanwhile, produces GPS systems working directly with OEMs; however, this division is in decline as it notes it has passed the peak of its partnership with BMW, and other legacy partnerships approach end of life.

The Issues: Steep Valuation, Deceleration, and Margin Compression

Now that we’ve covered Garmin’s business, we can dig more into the meat of my sell case for Garmin. My core underlying belief is this: though we applaud Garmin’s performance over the past year, especially in driving substantial increases in operating profit in the fitness segment, Garmin’s premium after its sharp rally over the past year is unsustainable.

At current share prices near $250, Garmin sits at a $49.45 billion market cap. We do note that Garmin has a substantial cash reserve: after netting out the $2.74 billion of cash on Garmin’s most recent balance sheet, the company’s resulting enterprise value is lower at $44.71 billion. A step-up in capital returns has been a core focus for Garmin recently (and a core reason the stock has quickly become a market darling); for 2026, the company is planning a 17% increase in its quarterly dividend to $1.05 per share (in spite of this, at the current ~1.7% yield, Garmin isn’t a rich dividend stock either). This is also on top of a fresh $500 million buyback plan (though after elevated share prices, this can only buy back ~1% of stock at today’s prices).

For FY26, as shown in the chart above, Garmin has guided to 9% y/y revenue growth to $7.90 billion in revenue. Issue number one is the fact that Garmin is expecting margin compression in FY26, in part due to two gross margin headwinds: tariffs plus higher memory costs. When pressed on memory in the Q&A portion of the Q4 earnings call, management didn't exactly break out how much of a headwind it expected memory to be, only that the company had secured supply agreements with key vendors. Per CEO Clifton Pemble's remarks:

Joe, I think in terms of quantifying the impact, we don't quantify individual components of our cost structure. So we won't be sharing that. Definitely, there's pressure on memory costs. There are certainly a lot of items in our overall BoM that are pricier items like displays and that kind of thing. So we simply just manage the entire BoM to be as cost efficient as possible. There's other opportunities to make the BoMs more efficient and also make our overall supply chain more efficient, looking for cost opportunities across the spectrum. So we're working all different angles, and there isn't 1 area to identify that we would isolate because it's the entire picture. I would remind everyone that our overall margin structure is higher, and that's because we're a vertically integrated company. And so therefore, when we see some variation at the BoM level, of course, the impact to the overall margin is less impactful."

Regardless, at an overall level, gross margins are down -20 bps y/y (from 58.7% in FY25), and operating margins are down -40 bps y/y (from 25.9% in FY26). The company is expecting to benefit from a lower 16.0% tax rate (versus 17.4% in FY25), which is positioning EPS at $9.35 (+9% y/y, in line with revenue growth).

Garmin's valuation multiples sit at:

  • 27.0x FY26 P/E
  • 24.4x FY26 ex-cash P/E

This is about a ~20% premium to the broader S&P 500, despite rather meager ~9% revenue and earnings growth (with margin compression essentially offset by a lower tax rate, which I view to be more of an exogenous factor rather than a true operating achievement). The key question investors need to ask now is, can Garmin truly continue to justify this premium as its growth is slowing?

Where I am concerned is that Garmin has relied heavily on the fitness watch segment to drive sales in FY25. In Q4, fitness revenue accelerated to 42% y/y growth.

This was driven, in part, by Garmin partnering with TrueMed beginning in October (Q4) to give customers the option to use HSA/FSA dollars to purchase their smartwatches . It's true that this could unlock more demand tailwinds in FY26 as using this benefit becomes more widespread.

That said, we also have to note that Garmin prides itself on rugged, long-lasting watches. Runners replace their shoes every 500 miles, but a good watch can last a very long time. Garmin is facing tough competition against very strong smartwatch sales in FY25, and it's unlikely that the same customers will be repeat buyers next year.

This is especially true when we stack on a tough macroeconomic environment. Key electronics retailers like Best Buy (BBY) have continued to warn investors about a tepid, promotionally driven demand environment. In other words, we should expect replacement cycles for Garmin products to elongate, which will impact its growth rates.

Again the question needs to be asked: how can we stomach a high-20s P/E multiple for a company that is expecting sales to decelerate to the single digits (or potentially worse if macro conditions tighten?)

Key Takeaways

Garmin has achieved tremendous sales growth over the past year, driven by rising popularity among fitness enthusiasts for its products. That said, I think its stock price and valuation multiples have raced far ahead of actual fundamentals. In FY26, we have to watch out for tough prior-year comps, a tough economy, and potentially slow watch upgrade cycles, compounded on top of an unfavorable margin environment driven by tariffs and sharply higher memory costs. To me, this stock is a clear sell.

data.title: UnitedHealth: Trust Is Broken - But The Model Isn't
data.description:
Something has clearly gone wrong at UnitedHealth (UNH). The key question is how permanent it is.

This is not a clean story, which is precisely why it is interesting. Following margin pressure in Medicare Advantage, weaker execution in Optum Health, and the withdrawal of guidance, confidence in the business has clearly been damaged.

In my view, the current situation reflects a temporary margin reset and not a permanent impairment. What matters now is whether the underlying drivers (especially the medical cost ratio) begin to stabilise.

If they do, the current setup offers an attractive risk/reward.

Company Overview

Over the past ten years, UnitedHealth has delivered remarkably consistent growth, with revenue increasing at a CAGR of approx. 10%. This was driven by expansion in Medicare Advantage and the rapid scaling of the Optum franchise.

This growth came to an abrupt end last year. Guidance was lowered, management changed, and concerns around billing practices emerged. For 2026, management is guiding for a revenue decline of approx. 2%, which would mark the first decline in over a decade. Margins are expected to recover but to remain below pre-2025 levels.

What Went Wrong

The recent underperformance is not driven by a single issue. Utilization in Medicare Advantage moved higher, while pricing lagged due to the backward-looking nature of insurance. At the same time, the transition to the V28 risk model added pressure on reimbursement, and execution in Optum Health fell short as growth outpaced costs.

What matters is not the individual factors, but the combination. These effects stacked on top of each other and pushed margins below their normal range. This ultimately broke investor confidence.

Why I Think This Is Fixable

Let's start with what is working.

Despite strong top-line growth over the past decade, UnitedHealth has consistently improved its operating cost efficiency. The operating cost ratio has steadily (although in small steps) declined. In my opinion this is remarkable, especially if we consider that the business grew by approximately 10% in most years. I view this as a strong indication that there is solid cost discipline in administrative and back-office functions.

As shown in the chart above, management is guiding for further improvement in 2026, which reinforces my view that internal cost control remains intact.

The cost pressure is clearly visible in the medical care ratio, which has increased significantly over the past two years. In my view, the key points the market underestimates are that this is largely cyclical and partially self-inflicted and therefore fixable.

Insurance Business

Pricing is already starting to reset.

  • ACA (commercial exchange market): This is where repricing is most visible. Premium increases in some regions exceed 20%, reflecting higher utilization and prior underpricing. Given the flexibility in the commercial market, insurers can adjust pricing relatively quickly to correct margins.
  • Medicare Advantage (MA): The situation is more challenging. Cost trends are currently running at ~5-7%, while CMS payment updates for 2027 are only ~2.5%.

I think it is important to distinguish between these two segments. While Medicare Advantage operates under regulated reimbursement updates, the ACA business is part of the commercial market, where pricing can be adjusted more dynamically.

However, this flexibility does not eliminate the underlying issue. Utilization trends have also come in stronger than expected in the ACA population, and pricing initially lagged these developments, now reflected in significant premium increases. What we are seeing now is a catch-up phase with insurers pushing through significant premium increases to restore profitability.

In Medicare Advantage, this adjustment mechanism is slower. Pricing is effectively set through annual bid cycles and limited by CMS reimbursement updates. As a result, cost inflation is not immediately offset fully.

At the same time, management is actively reducing exposure to unprofitable members. I view the decline in MA membership as a positive signal, as it reflects repricing and adjustments to improve profitability.

Optum Franchise

Optum Health margins have come under pressure, as shown in the chart above. The business expanded aggressively in recent years, but cost control and execution did not keep up with that pace.

Management is now shifting the focus away from growth towards better alignment between contracts, cost structure, and execution. This should support a gradual margin improvement.

The recent decline does not indicate a broken model, in my view. It reflects a period where growth outpaced operational discipline.

Group Level

At the group level, the investment case remains intact.

UnitedHealth benefits from its scale and integrated model, combining insurance with healthcare services and data capabilities through Optum. This has been built over decades and, in my view, is not impaired by one difficult cycle.

It is also important to keep the broader context in mind: a functioning healthcare system depends on a functioning insurance market. As the largest player, UnitedHealth plays a central role in that system.

I therefore view the current situation as a margin reset cycle and not a structural impairment.

Valuation

Based on current multiples, UnitedHealth does not appear obviously cheap. At around ~17x P/E (NTM), the stock is trading broadly in line with its historical range.

However, I think we need to interpret this very carefully.

I view the current valuation as a balance between two opposing views. On one hand, the market acknowledges the strength of the business (scale, integration, growing demand). On the other hand, recent developments have clearly reduced confidence in the company's ability to get back to prior margin levels.

The result is a "middle ground" valuation; not pricing a collapse, but also not a full recovery.

In my view, this caution may be too conservative.

Insurance profitability has historically been cyclical, with cost increases typically followed by pricing adjustments (although with a lag).

Same for membership growth. The current decline is often viewed as a sign of weakness. I view it as an intentional move to increase profitability.

Execution at Optum Health has clearly been disappointing. However, I don't see evidence that the model itself is broken. It reflects a period where growth outpaced operational discipline, which is now being corrected.

Overall, I view the current valuation as uncertainty around the recovery and not fully reflecting a normalization scenario.

Peer Comparison

A simple peer comparison needs to be interpreted with caution because the group is not homogeneous.

As shown in the chart above, valuation across the peer group differs a lot. Pure insurers such as Elevance Health (ELV) and Cigna (CI) trade at significantly lower multiples; more complex or Medicare-focused models show higher volatility (e.g., Humana (HUM)).

UnitedHealth sits in between. It keeps a premium to pure insurers but trades below prior peaks. This supports the view that the market is discounting uncertainty rather than pricing a breakdown.

This is where I differ from the market.

The current valuation appears reasonable if margins remain below prior levels. However, if pricing catches up and the medical care ratio stabilizes, margins do not need to fully recover to previous peaks for the stock to re-rate.

Even a partial normalization would be sufficient to support higher earnings and, in turn, a higher valuation multiple.

Risks

The main risk to this thesis is that current pressures prove more structural than expected.

This would be the case if healthcare utilization remains elevated for longer, pricing fails to catch up, or regulatory pressure on Medicare Advantage limits margin recovery. In addition, further execution issues in Optum Health or a longer loss of management credibility would delay or weaken the recovery.

Conclusion

This is not a clean story - and that is precisely why it is interesting.

The upcoming earnings release is less about quarterly performance and more about whether management can re-establish trust.

I view the current situation as a temporary margin reset and not as a broken business model. If the underlying drivers begin to stabilize, the current valuation leaves room for re-rating.

While the burden of proof now lies with the company, I view the risk/reward at current levels as attractive.

data.title: Credo: 'Optics Threat' Creates The Kind Of AI Buying Opportunity I Dream Of
data.description:
The AI trade is offering a buying opportunity due to ongoing market volatility. Credo (CRDO) looks particularly attractive as it offers direct exposure to ongoing data center growth. Investor fear regarding the long-term risks from optics looks overblown, with the stock trading at conservative multiples relative to forward growth rates. I am initiating coverage with a strong buy rating.

CRDO Stock Price

CRDO has been an absolute rocket ship since generative AI has taken off, as it has been a key supplier of the data center buildout. The stock has, however, seen considerable volatility over the last year or so.

Investors appear unconvinced that CRDO will remain a big winner over the long term.

CRDO Stock Key Metrics

CRDO is most known for being the leader in active electrical cables (AECs), which are used to help facilitate the transfer of data within data centers. The general idea is that traditional copper cables are highly limited by transmission distance, making AECs the industry standard for modern data centers, as they can support higher speeds at longer distances. It appears that CRDO stock has seen a re-rating lower, in large part due to rising optimism for optical players such as Lumentum (LITE) and Coherent (COHR), with Nvidia making significant investments into the names to accelerate innovation. I note that NVDA CEO Huang would then go on to reiterate the importance of AECs in the data center. In theory, the rise of optics could represent a real risk to CRDO, as in the optics bull case, lasers would be able to do what AECs can but for significantly larger distances, helping to support the next age of data centers. That, however, is still arguably theoretical, as present-day optics technology is still unable to provide that kind of performance without losing reliability or cost-effectiveness. I am of the view that the market may be looking too far ahead with regard to the potential risks to CRDO while also ignoring the company’s ability to reposition in the meantime.

CRDO has been a clear beneficiary of the rise of generative AI, with revenues surging exponentially over the last handful of years.

In the most recent quarter, CRDO delivered 201.5% YoY revenue growth to $407 million, which also represented 51.9% QoQ growth. CRDO has delivered incredible operating leverage, with its GAAP operating margin surging to over 30% year-to-date.

CRDO ended the quarter with $1.3 billion of cash versus no debt, representing a strong balance sheet.

Looking ahead, management expects the company to deliver up to $435 million in revenue in the fourth quarter, representing over 150% YoY growth.

On the conference call, management appeared well aware of the negative market sentiments and came out defiant, stating that “where you can use copper, you will use copper.” While the company is set to lap tough comparables, management still expects “more than 50% year-over-year growth” in the next year. Management noted that they have been “in development on ZF Optics for going on 2 years,” which would actually likely see them partnering with the supposed optics threat by effectively providing the brains of the lasers. For now, though, I expect the company to remain a strong beneficiary of the ongoing surge in data center spending.

Is CRDO Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

Due perhaps to investor fears regarding long term disruption risk from optics, CRDO is curiously one of the few AI stocks trading at reasonable valuations. The stock recently traded at just around 25x forward earnings.

That is a curiously attractive valuation given the projected 50% forward top line growth rate.

Of course, investors might be worried about the potential that AI is at a cyclical top - the similarly cheap valuations seen at Nvidia (NVDA) suggest this. I think a cyclical top is unlikely. Agentic AI looks inevitable and imminent given the rapidly expanding AI use cases. I would expect agentic AI to require significantly more compute. It is likely that CRDO does see growth rates decelerate, but I do expect consistent growth ahead as AI adoption accelerates. Even if one assigns more conservative growth multiples due to potential deceleration, the stock looks attractive. The current multiple implies a less than 1x price-to-earnings growth ratio (‘PEG ratio’). Assuming no multiple expansion may lead to 20% annual growth, as I expect solid growth beyond this year. It is worth noting that this is a company where insiders retain significant “skin in the game.”

While there are fears that optics might eventually displace AECs, those days appear to be still distant. If one believes that more data centers will be needed to support agentic AI, then CRDO stands to disproportionately benefit from its mission-critical function in facilitating communication.

CRDO Stock Risks

CRDO is highly concentrated to the hyperscalers, with its top 3 customers making up an astounding 88% of revenues.

If the hyperscalers move to another provider, then CRDO may see considerable pressure on its business. It is possible that the optics industry improves faster than expected. It is possible that the data center buildout stalls, due to oil costs or regulatory pushback.

CRDO Stock Conclusion

CRDO is an AI stock I can fully support. I do not see the same cyclicality concerns that I have seen elsewhere, as CRDO is a clear leader. AECs remain vital for the data center buildout, meaning CRDO is squarely positioned to benefit from the ongoing data center buildout. CRDO is the kind of stock I look for in the Best of Breed Growth Stocks Portfolio. I am initiating coverage with a strong buy rating.

data.title: Tesla Q1 Preview: It's About To Be Story Time On Wall Street
data.description:
Tesla, Inc. (TSLA) is heading into the Q1 2025 print, and the market is closely monitoring the fundamentals of the company, as the automotive business is under pressure.

Right?

Wrong.

In my view, that's what traditional fundamentals-based driven investors would say, particularly after the Q1 delivery miss.

Call me crazy, but based on the historical disconnect between fundamentals and price action for this stock, I think the shareholder base is not particularly concerned with the EV business, even though it accounted for 71% of total sales in Q4 2025.

In this piece, I discuss what I believe really matters for a narrative stock like Tesla heading into the Q1 2026 print.

Overall, I reiterate my hold rating heading into the print, as the two conditions that I believe matter for a narrative stock like Tesla are still not satisfied. Therefore, the risk of more downside ahead is significant, despite the 30% selloff since December.

Remember What Matters and What Ain't Heading into the Q1 Print

Fundamentals? No thanks, this is not a car company (it's what almost any analyst with a buy rating or shareholder would say, in my view).

Don't take it from me. I'm just a sub-blue-collar, high-risk speculator. Take it from no other than the CEO, Elon Musk:

If you value Tesla as just like an auto company, you just have to – fundamentally, it's just the wrong framework and it will come to be Q1 2024 earnings.

Since that statement, the stock has more than doubled in price. Sorry, but price is what matters at the end of the day if you are an investor (both long and short).

Therefore, I wouldn't focus that much on hard data points, like, for example, the fact that Tesla delivered only 358,023 vehicles in Q1 2026, below analysts’ 368,903 expectation. Or the fact that production exceeded deliveries by 50,363 vehicles.

Who cares about demand softness and inventory buildup in its automotive business, which accounts for 71% of total sales in Q4 2025?

Who's looking at the chart below anyway?

People want to hear stories. The more exciting, the better.

At this point, I'd like to refer (confused) fundamentals-based readers to my previous article on this story stock, titled "My Playbook On Trading Tesla As The Market Stops Rewarding Story Stocks (Rating Downgrade)."

Ok, so let's focus on the narrative.

Q1 2026 Preview

On the robotaxi timeline, the company is targeting fully autonomous vehicles in 25% to 50% of the United States by year-end 2026, pending regulatory approval, of course.

The fact that the company had a similar target last year and missed it terribly is not relevant at all, as the next story is more important than the last (remember my playbook). These were Elon's words back on July 23, 2025:

I think we will probably have autonomous ride-hailing in probably half the population of the U.S. by the end of the year

Moving on, the company said it would unveil Optimus 3 within a few months' time. That was back in January this year. A few months have passed, and nothing. Unsurprisingly enough, it was delayed.

I expect the next call to introduce a new timeline, so keep an eye out for this item during the upcoming earnings call. Not that it matters much, as Tesla stock was up mid-single digits on Elon's post on March 31.

So, what matters then?

It is my strongest view that a stock like Tesla, which is one of the Magnificent 7, owned by many respectable institutional funds (like Coatue Asset Management, a mostly tech fund I deeply respect), is a beta on the market's risk appetite.

Therefore, given the high institutional and retail participation, I observed that when the broader markets and the overall macro backdrop support risk-taking, stocks like Tesla tend to perform quite well, regardless of their fundamentals. In fact, there is a noticeable correlation between Bitcoin (IBIT in the chart below) and TSLA:

As I said before:

If you've been following my articles, you should know the number one rule in my playbook for story stocks: the narrative matters more than fundamentals when the macro supports risk-taking.

Admittedly, my earlier downgrade of Tesla was mainly based on macro factors, as I quickly noticed a shift away from risk assets once the conflict in the Middle East began.

Back to my Q1 preview, another item that I would closely monitor is the new FSD commercialization model. As you may remember, Vaibhav Taneja said in the last call that "beginning this quarter, we are transitioning fully to a subscription-based model for FSD."

For reference, FSD was available for a one-time purchase of $8,000 on Tesla’s order pages. From what I'm seeing on the sales page, the $8,000 one-time fee is not available anymore. Instead, there is a monthly subscription of $99.

I would listen for any feedback on this transition, as it appears to be a better model than the previous one-time fee option. That said, bear in mind that before, both options (subscription and one-time fee) were available. So I wouldn't expect a significant uptick in FSD subscriptions right away.

Finally, I would keep an eye on the CapEx planned for this year. Back in 2025, the company spent $8.5 billion in capital expenditures. The guidance for 2026 implies more than $20 billion, driven by AI compute infrastructure, manufacturing lines, and company-operated AI-enabled assets, according to the latest 10-K.

The problem with this guidance is the fact that in 2025, the company generated only $14.747 billion of operating cash flow. Assuming (big assumption, given the deterioration in the automotive business) a similar operating cash flow figure this year, free cash flows would be negative by $5.3 billion.

The question that readers need to ask is whether FCF matters for this stock. In my view, based on the $44.06 billion in cash + cash equivalents + short-term investments at the end of last quarter, the negative FCF topic is mostly off the table.

Looking Ahead

As I'm typing, Tesla is trading at 172x forward earnings. That's over 10x the median of the consumer discretionary sector. Is the valuation warranted?

In my view, the answer is yes, as long as two conditions are met.

First, Elon needs to spark the market's optimism with new, exciting goals and timelines. Whether the company actually meets these targets or not is secondary, based on the past misses without much implication on the share price.

Speaking of which, the stock is down 30% from the December highs and is trading below the 200-day moving average. As Paul Tudor Jones once said, nothing good happens below the 200-day moving average.

The second condition is the reopening of the Strait of Hormuz.

Right, because higher oil prices are pressuring the consumer's discretionary pockets, and therefore, they may not have the budget to buy a Tesla, even though they run on batteries? (some may say)

Cute. No. In my view, that's not the real reason. The market is closely awaiting the reopening of the Strait of Hormuz to go back to risk-on mode. Some participants with high-risk appetites (like myself) had already bought the dip last week after the ceasefire was announced. However, as I'm writing, this headline just came in.

That's not the best news for risk assets (remember my argument on who owns Tesla), even though I believe the two will make a deal before the end of the ceasefire.

Therefore, I would rather remain on the sidelines heading into the Q1 print, as I see too much noise in the broader markets, and the technicals don't warrant anything more than a hold, in my view.

data.title: 6 Barbell Stocks: Growth Meets Income For U.S. And Iran Negotiations
data.description:
Markets Stabilize, But Uncertainty Calls for Diversification

Markets have found short-term relief with the two-week ceasefire in the Iran war, easing immediate geopolitical risk, and helping to stabilize investor sentiment. Combined with what is expected to be a mostly positive quarterly earnings season, the current backdrop could support select stocks in the near term, especially those with solid fundamental strength and growth potential. Friday’s slightly softer than expected CPI also helps the case for growth stocks. However, the broader macro environment remains fragile as the ultimate resolution of the Iran war is still uncertain and oil supply disruptions can cause ongoing negative ripple effects, keeping gas prices and inflation elevated. Midterm elections can also add another layer of uncertainty, potentially driving further market swings in the months ahead.

Mixing Semis, Energy, Industrials and Real Estate With Oil

In this environment, where upside catalysts coexist with persistent downside risks, a barbell approach has worked well for us in the past. A mix that blends high-risk growth with lower-risk, yield-producing, defensive positions can achieve a strong barbell portfolio. This offers a way to participate in gains while reducing overall volatility when combined into a portfolio.

Best Stocks to Buy Now: A Barbell Approach

To select the best stocks to feature in this article, I used the Seeking Alpha Stock Screener and targeted the highest Quant-Rated Strong Buy stocks in the respective sector targets. The sectors include technology, energy, infrastructure-focused industrials, and real estate:

  • High-quality tech captures upside from earnings growth strength and AI-driven demand.
  • Energy provides a hedge against geopolitical risk and inflation while offering high yields.
  • Industrials add earnings visibility through backlog-driven demand.
  • REITs offer rate-sensitive upside and income, benefiting from stabilizing rates and resilient property-level cash flows.

Together, they balance cyclical growth and defensive qualities, allowing investors to navigate volatility while remaining positioned for multiple market outcomes.

1. Sandisk Corporation (SNDK)

  • Market Capitalization: $125.69B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 04/10/2026): 1 out of 530

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 04/10/2026): 1 out of 26

  • Sector: Information Technology

  • Industry: Technology Hardware, Storage, and Peripherals

A holding in my Pro Quant Portfolio, Sandisk is an American multinational technology company operating in the data storage segment of the semiconductor industry, benefiting from growth demand for NAND flash driven by AI workloads, cloud computing, and enterprise storage needs. As a high-beta tech stock, it fits firmly into the growth side of the barbell strategy. In the current macro environment, where earnings strength can offset geopolitical uncertainty, Sandisk's exposure to data growth provides a strong tailwind.

Despite a stunning near 2,228% price jump over the past year, SNDK still amazingly has an attractive valuation when factoring in its forward growth. The company’s forward non-GAAP P/E of 20.91 offers more than a 6% discount to the sector median, while its forward PEG of 0.09 reveals a 93% discount to sector peers. Like many of the high-beta stocks I spotlight, SNDK’s attractive valuation can be justified by key metrics that give it an 'A+' grade for its Quant Growth Factor, but the growth story is told through Sandisk’s incredible margin expansion, which is expected to continue.

In its Q2 fiscal year 2026 earnings report, Sandisk crushed estimates as non-GAAP EPS of $6.20 beat by $2.66 and revenue of $3.03B (+61.2% Y/Y) beat by $340M. Expectations are for more of the same for the next quarter’s earnings to be reported April 30. SNDK’s growth story and momentum make it a prime example of a recovery pick that enhances its appeal for capturing risk-on sentiment when it returns in full force. From this high-growth starting point, the portfolio turns toward another semiconductor name with even greater leverage to AI-driven demand.

2. Micron Technology, Inc. (MU)

  • Market Capitalization: $475.35B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 04/10/2026): 3 out of 530

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 04/10/2026): 1 out of 69

  • Sector: Information Technology

  • Industry: Semiconductors

Also a PQP holding and in my Alpha Picks portfolio, Micron Technology designs and manufactures memory and storage solutions, positioning it at the center of the AI and data center buildout. Its DRAM and NAND products are critical to AI’s high performance demands, giving it strong earnings visibility as the hyperscale buildout continues. Within the barbell framework, Micron combines high-beta exposure with strong fundamentals, particularly through upward earnings revisions and expanding profit margins. From a Quant perspective, those expanding margins enable the earnings growth that in turn creates an attractive valuation profile.

Starting with MU’s forward non-GAAP P/E of 7.29x earnings, investors are getting a 67.31% discount to the sector median. After factoring in its forward growth, the 0.06 PEG reveals more than a 95% discount to sector peers. With DRAM and NAND shortages expected into 2027, MU is in a good position to continue expanding margins that support its forward growth, which earns MU an A+ Factor Grade.

With forward EPS Growth expected at more than 415% and Operating Cash Flow Growth at more than 128%, Micron’s ability to scale remains in place as demand from giants like Nvidia (NVDA) will only grow, further amplifying the factors driving margin growth. As markets weigh earnings strength against geopolitical and inflation risks, MU offers a powerful growth driver, which is balanced next by a stock that can benefit directly from those same macro pressures.

3. Riley Exploration Permian (REPX)

  • Market Capitalization: $843.72M

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 04/10/2026): 19 out of 226

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 04/10/2026): 3 out of 68

  • Sector: Energy

  • Industry: Oil and Gas Exploration and Production

Riley Exploration Permian is an upstream oil and gas company domestically focused on production in the Permian Basin, which is located in the southwestern region of the U.S. Its direct exposure to oil prices makes REPX a natural hedge against geopolitical instability and supply disruptions associated with ongoing tensions in the Middle East. Although some investors may prefer large-cap energy companies like Chevron (CVX) or Exxon Mobil (XOM), Riley Exploration Permian stands to benefit more from higher energy prices, which can come with inflation and global uncertainty. Strong free cash flow, a 4.09% yield, and strong recent performance momentum enhance its appeal. Topping off the attraction from a Quant perspective, I like what I see in the company’s key valuation metrics, which tell me there’s room to run for the stock price.

While REPX fits in our barbell mix as an income-producing oil price and inflation hedge, it’s also a growth story that begins with an attractive valuation. The company’s forward non-GAAP P/E of 7.45 offers a 45% discount to the sector median, while its growth-adjusted PEG ratio supports that valuation as it offers more than a 47% discount to sector peers. While REPX’s 'C+' Growth Factor grade may appear lackluster on the surface, the forward trend looks healthy.

The growth grade for REPX has improved from a ‘D-’ to a ‘C+’ over the past three months. Helping to explain this improvement is a positive transition from shrinking revenue (-4.44% YoY) to Forward Revenue projections of 12.40%, which is 170% ahead of the sector median. Supporting this growth, Riley Permian forecasts more than 20% oil volume growth in 2026 while accelerating capital deployment and buybacks. While growth stocks can thrive in a risk-on environment, REPX can provide balance by performing when those conditions deteriorate, which leads us to another energy stock that can supplement this portion of our barbell.

4. SM Energy Company (SM)

  • Market Capitalization: $6.75B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 04/10/2026): 28 out of 226

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 04/10/2026): 7 out of 68

  • Sector: Energy

  • Industry: Oil and Gas Exploration and Production

SM Energy is a U.S.-based exploration and production company focused on oil-rich assets in the Permian Basin and Eagle Ford, positioning it to directly benefit from higher oil prices. In the current macro environment, marked by geopolitical uncertainty around the Iran war and ongoing supply disruptions, SM can benefit from wider margins amid elevated oil prices. The company also has solid fundamentals, generating strong free cash flow, which enables it to offer a nice yield of 3.11%. From a Quant perspective, SM stands out with an attractive valuation and rapidly improving growth and profitability factors (moving from 'D+' to 'B' and 'D-' to 'B' for its respective grades).

To highlight the company’s growth improvement, forward Revenue Growth is expected at over 39%, which is more than double its YoY growth. Forward EBITDA Growth is 35.40%, which is nearly 593% higher than the sector median. Shifting over to its profitability strength, SM’s EBITDA Margin of nearly 73% is significantly more than double the company’s sector peers.

This combination of improving fundamentals and macro tailwinds makes SM a compelling addition to the barbell’s more aggressive side while still serving the income generation and inflationary hedge roles. From energy’s cyclical upside, we now turn to an industrial stock that offers more stable, backlog-driven infrastructure growth potential.

5. Sterling Infrastructure, Inc. (STRL)

  • Market Capitalization: $13.37B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 04/10/2026): 23 out of 610

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 04/10/2026): 2 out of 36

  • Sector: Industrials

  • Industry: Construction and Engineering

Another Alpha Pick holding, Sterling Infrastructure is an engineering and construction company focusing on infrastructure, e-infrastructure, and transportation projects. Its exposure to data center development, power infrastructure, and public works creates a unique hybrid profile within the barbell strategy. Backlog-driven revenue growth provides earnings visibility, while electrification trends and AI power demand support long-term growth. Unlike traditional cyclicals, Sterling’s business is less dependent on consumer spending, making it more resilient in uncertain economic conditions.

Sterling is a growth story, and its 2026 potential is supported by management’s expectation to reach 25% revenue growth for the year as backlog and data center demand surge. Supportive Quant metrics include forward EBIT Growth of more than 30%, which is more than 3x the sector median, and forward EPS Long Term Growth (3-5Y CAGR) at 19%, offering a 54.38% premium to sector peers. Meanwhile, Sterling’s sector-leading return on capital of over 19% indicates the company is doing an outstanding job of turning invested capital into profits relative to its peers.

Sterling’s combination of growth and profit stability helps bridge the gap between the portfolio’s higher-risk and defensive components, making it the perfect way to tie up our broader barbell strategy of balancing opportunity with protection.

6. DiamondRock Hospitality Company (DRH)

  • Market Capitalization: $2.07B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 04/10/2026): 4 out of 170

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 04/10/2026): 1 out of 17

  • Sector: Real Estate

  • Industry: Hotel & Resort REITs

DiamondRock Hospitality Company is a lodging REIT focused on premium hotels and resorts in top U.S. markets. This positions DRH to benefit from continued strength from more affluent consumers that have held up travel demand and support pricing power. Unlike some traditional REITs that are facing challenges, hospitality remains strong in the consumer segment. As I mentioned in a recent article, Top 3 REITs For Reliable Income In Volatile Markets, the political and economic backdrop warrants caution for the real estate sector as a whole. However, DRH sees several favorable factors supporting a positive outlook in 2026, including key markets hosting the majority of FIFA World Cup matches, America 250 celebrations, and post-renovation tailwinds.

DRH’s healthy 'B-' Valuation Factor grade is supported by multiple favorable metrics, especially its forward P/AFFO of 10.06, which offers nearly a 35% discount to the sector median. This provides evidence that DRH is trading well below the value of its cash flows, offering an element of stability for investors. This discounted valuation also leaves room for price appreciation as sentiment improves when rates stabilize. As a result, investors can benefit from DRH’s solid 3.97% yield, as supported by its 'A+' Dividend Safety Grade.

In the current macro environment, DRH adds a rate-sensitive element to the barbell mix, as REIT valuations tend to improve when interest rates stabilize or decline. The combination of potential upside and rate sensitivity makes DRH an addition to the mix, rounding out the portfolio before we turn to the broader conclusion.

Conclusion: Barbell Offers Best Stocks for "Time In" Markets

In a market driven by geopolitical uncertainty, inflation pressures, and shifting interest rate expectations, a barbell strategy offers a disciplined way to navigate volatility. Rather than timing the market, this approach enables "time in" the market by remaining exposed with a diversified mix. By combining high-growth semiconductor stocks with energy exposure, infrastructure industrials, and select REITs, this portfolio offers multiple ways to win with balance. Each position serves a role, whether through earnings-driven upside, macro hedging, income stability, or rate sensitivity. Rather than relying on a single market outcome, this approach allows investors to remain flexible, participating in gains while maintaining resilience in a range of economic scenarios.

data.title: Ultragenyx Pharmaceutical: The Market Is Overlooking 2026 Catalysts
data.description:
It has been nearly a year and a half since my last Ultragenyx Pharmaceutical (RARE) article, where I discussed the company’s Q3 earnings report that showed a 42% year-over-year revenue increase, which encouraged me to finally commit to a starter position in RARE. I believed that the company’s late-stage pipeline could provide multiple catalysts for future growth, including Setrusumab and DTX401. At that time, Ultragenyx had a strong cash position, and I saw a path to profitability by 2026, which could allow the company to avoid the need for extensive dilutive financing. Since then, RARE is down roughly 50% after the company received a complete response letter (CRL) from the FDA for UX111 in Sanfilippo syndrome type A, then Setrusumab for osteogenesis imperfecta failed to hit the primary endpoint in two late-stage trials, and the company’s guidance fell short of the consensus. As a result, I decided to hold off on adding to my position.

However, I was reading a recent investors conference transcript and realized that my Ultragenyx thesis is still intact, as the company leverages their commercial products (Crysvita, Dojolvi, Mepsevii, and Evkeeza) to provide a financial base to help fund their impressive late-stage pipeline that could move the company closer to profitability. It appears that 2026 is going to be a pivotal year for Ultragenyx with multiple data readouts, regulatory milestones, and potential approvals that could bolster investor sentiment and improve RARE’s long-term prospects. As a result, I’m keeping a close eye on the ticker for an opportunity to add to my dormant position.

I intend to review the company’s current commercial foundation and the list of potential catalysts on the horizon. Then, I will take a look at the commercial implications of these programs and their potential impact on the company’s valuation. In addition, I will point out a few downside risks that investors should be aware of when managing their RARE position. Finally, I take a look at the daily chart to see if I can identify an opportunity to add to my position.

Underrated Commercial Foundation

When catching up on Ultragenyx’s current condition, I was really surprised that a rare disease biotech pulled in $673M last year, which was up 20% from 2024. Digging deeper, it looks as if Crysvita was responsible for $481M of that $673M. Meanwhile, Dojolvi brought in $96M, Mepsevii $37M, and Evkeeza experienced 84% growth year-over-year to log $59M.

So, I would say that Ultragenyx is capable of handling multiple launches in different rare disease arenas while still pushing their late-stage programs closer to the finish line. This commercial groundwork could provide enough stability for the company to make their push towards their goal of profitability by 2027.

Catalyst Timeline

Ultragenyx has several potent clinical catalysts slated in 2026 and 2027, including readouts from their Angelman syndrome program, Glycogen Storage Disease Type Ia (GSDIa), and Sanfilippo syndrome, as well as Ornithine Transcarbamylase (OTC) deficiency and Wilson’s disease.

GTX-102 is Ultragenyx’s Angelman syndrome program and is one of their most anticipated catalysts, with two Phase III trials. It is important to note that Angelman syndrome does not have an approved therapy that has disease-modifying ability. So, there is a big opportunity for Ultragenyx to be the first mover and expand the market. In fact, one analyst believes that GTX-102 could have peak sales of $1.2B.

DTX401 is Ultragenyx’s gene therapy for GSDIa, which has a PDUFA date set in August 2026 after their Phase III trial showed improved glucose control and reduced dietary dependence. A Bank of America analyst has DTX401’s peak sales at about $796M, which would be impressive for therapy coming from a population of roughly 6K patients.

Ultragenyx’s UX111 for Sanfilippo syndrome could be another breakthrough drug because there is a lack of approved therapies. UX11’s cognitive and biomarker marks still have my approval. The company announced that the FDA has set a PDUFA date for September 19th, 2026.

The company’s DTX301 for the OTC program targets a rare urea cycle disorder, while UX701 targets Wilson’s disease. Both programs are in Phase III or are in pre-pivotal stages, with potentially important readouts over the next 12 months.

I would suggest reviewing the point above quickly one more time and understanding that we are looking at multiple Ultragenyx programs that are approaching FDA approval with breakthrough therapy designations. So, we could see Ultragenyx report approval across multiple programs, which could lead to multiple launches over the next few years, resulting in significant revenue growth going into the next decade.

Conservative Commercial Implications

I will concede that attempting to determine peak sales for rare disease and gene therapy products is incredibly murky because we don't really know what the market is for the product until it actually hits the market. We can look at patient populations and the market size to estimate the commercial opportunity, but penetration and prices are always a big question mark because these therapies are novel, so we don't know if there is demand for patients to try something completely new, and we don't know if payers are going to fully support the therapy. So, I tend to be cautious when trying to determine the valuation of rare disease pipelines.

I’m going to attempt to calculate very conservative peak estimates for Ultragenyx’s late-stage programs.

When trying to determine Ultragenyx’s late-stage pipeline valuation, I will be modeling using only the U.S. market with a 30% peak penetration and a conservative estimate of pricing based on other established rare-disease products. Under this conservative approach, I calculated that Ultragenyx has an aggregate $1.5B-$2.4B in peak annual revenue potential from these programs in the United States alone. So, not counting for ex-U.S. expansion or a broader label penetration.

GTW-102 for Angelman syndrome looks to be the largest single opportunity in the U.S. pipeline. With an estimated 6K to 10K treatable patients when I adjust for clinical eligibility constraints and only 30% peak penetration at an estimated $300K per patient (Spinraza is around $350K per year), I get a peak revenue range of $540M to $900M.

The second-largest opportunity could be DTX301 with OTC deficiency, with 800 to 1.2K treatable U.S. patients, and assuming a $1.5M one-time gene therapy price (Zolgensma is $2.1M) and 30% penetration, which gives us peak sales of $360M to $540M. DTX401 could add $210M to $300M in U.S. peak sales, assuming 350 to 500 eligible patients and a $2M price tag. In addition, UX701 could give Ultragenyx 5K-8K treatable U.S. patients with chronic therapies around $125K per year, which would be about $190M-$300M in peak sales at 30% penetration.

UX111 for Sanfilippo syndrome might be the smallest in absolute U.S. population, but the lack of approved disease-modifying therapies could make it a lucrative opportunity for Ultragenyx. With approximately 250 to 600 eligible U.S. patients and applying 30% penetration with a $2M price tag (Zolgensma comp), we get a peak sales range from $150M to $360M.

I know these estimates aren't very rosy, but I'm deliberately modeling them with some bearish assumptions. I did this to illustrate that Ultragenyx still has a lot to gain even if all of these programs achieve modest penetration, which could still yield a multi-billion-dollar expansion in their revenue base over time.

I’m not the only one with an optimistic outlook. It appears Street analysts are also expecting the company to report strong double-digit growth over the next 10 years, going from roughly $770M in 2026 to about $3.75B in 2035.

What is more, Street analysts are projecting Ultragenyx to report similar EPS growth, going from -4.40 in 2026 to 12.21 in 2035.

The industry’s average price-to-sales is roughly 4x-5x, so assuming Ultragenyx were to pull in roughly $3.75B in 2035, we could be looking at about $152 per share. RARE is currently trading around $23 per share, so there is significant upside if all goes well for Ultragenyx over the next 10 years.

Risks to Consider

Certainly, Ultragenyx has significant downside risks to consider when managing your position. I would first highlight the hallmark risks that come with being a rare disease company. We have to recognize that some of these programs are targeting very small patient populations, so a small network of treatment centers might impact how quickly patients can get to a center and be treated. So, it is possible that we see some of these programs get off to a slow start, despite there being a clear need.

Another risk to consider is that some of Ultragenyx’s programs are gene therapies, which carry plenty of risks, including the complexity of administration. AAV-based gene therapies often require the patient to go through pre-screening, preconditioning of their immune system, and post-treatment monitoring. Just adding these steps could limit site capacity and throughput. So, even if there is demand, it is possible that Ultragenyx’s therapies won't be able to scale fast enough early in the commercial launch.

We also have to acknowledge that the rare disease addressable market can be impacted by a lag in diagnosis and referrals. As a result, the real-world treatable population could be much smaller than the estimated market derived from prevalence statistics. The company can launch education and support programs to help improve genetic testing and newborn screening, but they are still relying on other parties to execute.

Perhaps the most concerning is the payer dynamics of rare diseases, which can add a lot of friction. Even when we are dealing with orphan indications, payers appear to be reluctant to really support these therapies. Yes, rare disease therapies pose a big risk for payers because they are flipping the bill for therapies with big price tags but limited real-world data. So, even if Ultragenyx gets these therapies approved, payers might require a prior authorization, ask for restrictive eligibility, and require a reimbursement agreement. Any of those hurdles could delay access to the company’s therapies and hurt the early stages of a commercial launch.

We also have to acknowledge the potential threat from competition. Although most of Ultragenyx’s programs have limited competition, we have to anticipate that a competitor will challenge these products at some point down the road. This could force Ultragenyx to adjust their prices substantially, which might result in a significant loss in revenue.

I also want to stress that having so many potent catalysts on the docket could lead to elevated volatility as the market attempts to digest the likelihood of approval and commercial success. RARE has a history of the share price being essentially cut in half following weak data or regulatory setbacks. So, investors need to accept that their position could have big swings in value over the next couple of years as the company’s current pipeline attempts to get through the FDA. Keep in mind that any setback or failure could generate doubts about the company’s financial health and timeline to break even. The company did finish 2025 with $680M in cash, but that cash position could quickly evaporate as the company maintains several late-stage programs and commercial launch prep. Plus, the company has $1.25B in debt, so the threat of dilution could grow if the company doesn’t have a strong winning percentage over the next 2 years.

Last but not least, Ultragenyx might have to deal with regulatory uncertainty and macroeconomic factors. Healthcare costs continue to rise, and scrutiny of the regulatory approval process for novel treatment options might lead to harsh ramifications for Ultragenyx's future operations.

Despite these risks, I'm maintaining my RARE conviction rating at 3 out of 5, and the ticker will remain in the Compounding Healthcare "Bio Boom" speculative portfolio
